title: Add glob support to podman run/create --mount
body:
HPC Community asked for this support specifically for using GPUs
within containers. Nvidia requires the correct shared library to
to be present in the directory that matches the device mounted
into the container. These libraries have random suffixes based
on versions of the installed libraries on the host.

podman run --mount type=glob:src=/usr/lib64/nvidia\*:ro=true. This helps
quadlets be more portable for this use case.

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
Podman --mount option supports bind mounts passed as globs.
```

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *rhatdan* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
@vrothberg @mheon @Luap99 @baude @containers/podman-maintainers PTAL

> I much rather see this as new option instead if we really need this

I like this idea.  The docs will be much easier to brain parse.

I do not agree that this is super confusing, more of a corner case. BTW I don't like -v /foo creating an anonymous directory, I would have thought it as a short hand of -v /foo:/foo, so I found the existing issue confusing.  I think /foo versus /foo* is looking for a corner case, which is unlikely to confuse real users.  

We already have --mount  and --volume.  I don't think we should add a --glob or -vglob or some other option to make it harder to discover the option.

If you want to force this on --mount, I guess I would be ok with that.  But I would prefer this just enhance --volume.

But is a breaking change. We cannot know how it is used today and we claim docker compatibility so this will hurt us in the long run. Corner cases are hard to document, hard to understand for users and hard to debug for us. We already have enough issues so I don't want to add problems that could have been easily avoided. 

Using `--mount` sounds possible, we could add a new type like `type=glob` for that.

I've been trying to find fault with this, because you know how I am, and I can't.  I can't think of any situation in which a volume name would contain asterisk, question mark, or bracket. And even if there are, for the first two, this code is a superset: `foo*` and `foo?` (actual asterisk and question mark in on-disk file) will still get mounted with `-v "foo*"/"foo?"` (asterisk/question mark in command line). I'm not seeing much possibility for confusion here.

I also tested with spaces, colons, semicolons, and everything behaved as it should.

Please squash commits though.

> I've been trying to find fault with this, because you know how I am, and I can't. I can't think of any situation in which a volume name would contain asterisk, question mark, or bracket. And even if there are, for the first two, this code is a superset: foo* and foo? (actual asterisk and question mark in on-disk file) will still get mounted with -v "foo*"/"foo?" (asterisk/question mark in command line). I'm not seeing much possibility for confusion here.


This is not what the current code does, a single path means create a anonymous volume and mount it at path in container.
The path is never used on the host. This PR complete changes that meaning if there is a glob character all of the sudden it means bind mount the path at the same place into the container.

```
# with this PR's code:
$ bin/podman run -v '/b*' -d --name c3  alpine top
# this creates volumes like this
{
                    "Type": "bind",
                    "Source": "/boot",
                    "Destination": "/boot",
                    "Driver": "",
                    "Mode": "",
                    "Options": [
                         "rbind"
                    ],
                    "RW": true,
                    "Propagation": "rprivate"
               },
               {
                    "Type": "bind",
                    "Source": "/bin",
                    "Destination": "/bin",
                    "Driver": "",
                    "Mode": "",
                    "Options": [
                         "rbind"
                    ],
                    "RW": true,
                    "Propagation": "rprivate"
               }

# now with this code when there is no match
$ bin/podman run -v '/z*' -d --name c3  alpine top
{
                    "Type": "volume",
                    "Name": "d057b9602f5675e58343d086604cb94d2358e685e12946188ba35d21ee17d042",
                    "Source": "/home/pholzing/.local/share/containers/storage/volumes/d057b9602f5675e58343d086604cb94d2358e685e12946188ba35d21ee17d042/_data",
                    "Destination": "/z*",
                    "Driver": "local",
                    "Mode": "",
                    "Options": [
                         "nosuid",
                         "nodev",
                         "rbind"
                    ],
                    "RW": true,
                    "Propagation": "rprivate"
               }
```

How is that not anything but confusing.

And then the big problem that this will break any user who uses these glob chars, linux allows all chars except NULL in the path so there can be users who use that. We must stay backwards compatible.
Note that the golang glob API also matches `[]`, saying people do not use these chars is just incorrect.



This is just a small example on my laptop, there are clearly applications using such characters.
```
$ find $HOME -name '*\]*' 2>/dev/null 
/home/pholzing/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/3b33e9bd1fc97cc11f568c5fe7e8e1ecc78ffe206fee45da10ca9493852cc017/diff/usr/share/fonts/google-noto-vf/NotoSans-Italic[wght].ttf
/home/pholzing/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/3b33e9bd1fc97cc11f568c5fe7e8e1ecc78ffe206fee45da10ca9493852cc017/diff/usr/share/fonts/google-noto-vf/NotoSans[wght].ttf
/home/pholzing/.debug/[kernel.kallsyms]
/home/pholzing/.debug/[vdso]
/home/pholzing/.thunderbird/b9aviz50.default-release/ImapMail/imap.gmail.com/[Gmail].msf
/home/pholzing/.thunderbird/b9aviz50.default-release/ImapMail/imap.gmail.com/[Gmail].sbd

$ find $HOME -name '*\?*' 2>/dev/null 
/home/pholzing/.cache/gnome-software/screenshots/624x351/71563fc1b6039a3b13d54bc1da929c756c5896a196ee5db72746d3f6f47194f5-FileRoller?action=AttachFile&do=get&target=file-roller.png
/home/pholzing/.cache/gnome-software/screenshots/112x63/71563fc1b6039a3b13d54bc1da929c756c5896a196ee5db72746d3f6f47194f5-FileRoller?action=AttachFile&do=get&target=file-roller.png
/home/pholzing/.cache/gnome-software/screenshots/752x423/71563fc1b6039a3b13d54bc1da929c756c5896a196ee5db72746d3f6f47194f5-FileRoller?action=AttachFile&do=get&target=file-roller.png

```

And then this here is just straight up broken:
```
$ bin/podman run -v '/e[' alpine true
Error: syntax error in pattern
```
Mainly because your example is a totally made up strange contrived example, that would never happen.  But if you want to argue it for the sake of winning an argument, you are right.
Would it be more sensible to require a destination path? `-v /var/lib/nvidia*:/var/lib:Z` remove potential anonymous-volume ambiguity and lets us alter the destination of the mounts if we want to.
How would that handle multiple matches.
Throw them all in the destination path. /var/lib/nvidia1 to $DESTDIR/nvidia1, /var/lib/nvidia2 to $DESTDIR/nvidia2

Paul will argue this is also a breaking change. since 
-v /var/lib/nvidia*:/var/lib:Z
Will currently take the /var/lib/nvidia\* and mount it on /var/lib.

I'm really liking Matt's destination-required idea. It adds what I think is an important flexibility that was missing from the original implementation:
```console
$ ... -v $HOME/test/volume*:/var/lib:Z ...
```
I still don't understand the problem with asterisk/questionmark, because this feature is a superset of current behavior (see my comment above). Brackets, I can see, but I wonder if this would solve it?
```go
    if _, err := Stat(splitVol[0]); err != nil {
        ...treat splitVol[0] as an actual true existing real thing
    } else {
        try globbing it...
```
I do think we should come up with a truth-table of scenarios, though, and run it through both current & proposed podman:
* `-v TMP/foo*:/tmp/foo`   where there are no files matching `fooanything`
* `-v TMP/foo*:/tmp/foo` where there are files or directories foo1, foo2
* `-v TMP/foo*:/tmp/foo` where there is an actual file called foo-star in addition to foo1 foo2
* .....
Maybe `-v /var/lib/nvidia*:/var/lib/*` with the trailing * on the destination meaning "in this directory" instead a literal mount to /var/lib?

First reaction: cringe, because seeing a star in a non-expansion context seriously gives me the heebie-jeebies. But I can breathe past that, because it's a valid idea. 

Second reaction: what does `/var/lib/nvidia*:/var/lib/*` mean? My eye finds it ambiguous:

| host | container, reasonable interpretation | container, mostly-but-not-entirely-unreasonable interpretation |
| ------ | ------ | ------ |
| /var/lib/nvidia1 | /var/lib/nvidia1 | /var/lib/1 |
| /var/lib/nvidia-foo | /var/lib/nvidia-foo | /var/lib/-foo |

Just a little bit of thinking convinces me that the right-hand column is silly, but a naïve reader seeing the above `-v` statement could reasonably be confused and have to spend time making sure that podman does the right thing. And then there's `-v /var/lib/nvidia*:/var/lib/nvidia/*` where I would genuinely feel baffled, because `/var/lib/nvidia/1` would be a reasonable interpretation of the right-hand wildcard. (Expectation-wise, not coding-wise).

I'm commenting in hopes of finding a good solution; this is not intended as a shoot-down.

Of course my example is made up but the fact is that not allowing these charters is a breaking change if people try to mount such paths, currently we only block `:` which is already resulted in bug reports because people want to mount such paths. While I agree it is unlikely to use such chars in paths I still don't think that justifies the risk. And keep in mind some paths will just result in a random `syntax error in pattern` errors. 

And then I don't agree that a user can understand that
- `-v /path` means anonymous volume
- `-v /path*` means anonymous volume (assuming that glob does not match)
-  `-v /path*` means bind mount of `/path...` to `/path...` when the glob matches something

So that just does not make sense to me and does not match the documented syntax of `[[SOURCE-VOLUME|HOST-DIR:]CONTAINER-DIR[:OPTIONS]]`

As for using `-v /var/lib/nvidia*:/var/lib/*` I think a better alternative would be just `-v /var/lib/nvidia*:` where you leave the right side empty and when you need options then `-v /var/lib/nvidia*::ro`, this would result in better consistency as the left path is always on the host. And as the container path currently cannot be empty we can be 100% sure to not break anyone as this syntax was not allowed previously.


But I really do not see the advantage of trying to get this into --volume. Using --mount with a new type or a completely new option sounds much better to me. As for discoverability you can mention such option directly in the --volume paragraph. I don't see how this would be any worse.

I will move to the --mount method. Regrettably this is way less used, but at least for HPC community and anyone wanting to use devices or libraries, where the paths are not static, it makes it harder to deal with. 
The interesting point when we go to implement this in quadlet.  Currently Mount= does not exists, just Volume.

SGTM

I agree with the points Paul made.  The nice thing, however, in using volumes is that it's already being supported in containers.conf.  This means we need to consider adding a `Mounts = [""]` field (or sth similar) to containers.conf to meet the HPC needs.
@mheon @edsantiago @vrothberg @giuseppe @flouthoc @Luap99 PTAL
Code LGTM. Would appreciate more eyes on manpages though.
I triggered the merge, and can deal with the man page changes in a CI:DOC update.
Followup https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/19406
